Sclareol具有抗炎作用，可能通过COX的抑制通路：体内和计算机研究.pdf

Page 1

Pharmaceutical Science Advances 2 (2024) 100029

Sclareol exerts an anti-inﬂammatory effect, possibly through COXs inhibition pathway: In vivo and in silico studies Abdullah Al Shamsh Prottay a, Mehedi Hasan Bappi a, Md Showkoth Akbor a, Aﬁa Ibnath Asha b, Md Shimul Bhuia a, Aqib Adnan Shaﬁn c, Md Nayem Mia a, Mohammad S. Mubarak d,**, Micheline de Azevedo Lima e, Henrique Douglas Melo Coutinho f,*, Muhammad Torequl Islam a a Department of Pharmacy, Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman Science and Technology University, Gopalganj, 8100, Bangladesh b Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman Science and Technology University, Gopalganj, 8100, Bangladesh c Department of Applied Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman Science and Technology University, Gopalganj, 8100, Bangladesh d Department of Chemistry, The University of Jordan, Amman, 11942, Jordan e Department of Molecular Biology, Federal University of Paraiba–UFPB, Jo~ao Pessoa, 58051-900, Paraiba, Brazil f Department of Biological Chemistry, Regional University of Cariri–URCA, Crato, 63105-000, Ceara, Brazil A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T

Keywords: Inﬂammation Sclareol Cyclooxygenase Anti-Inﬂammatory effect Molecular docking simulation

1. Introduction Inﬂammation is a widespread phenomenon that arises in major disturbances of homeostasis such as infection, injury, and exposure to pollutants. It is caused by innate immune receptors that identify pathogens and wounded cells [1]. In most cases, this process leads to infection recovery and healing of other tissues [2]. Chronic tissue damage caused by

Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Pharmaceutical Science Advances journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/pharmaceutical-science-advances

Chronic and severe inﬂammation results in many diseases and disorders in humans. Currently, available conventional anti-inﬂammatory drugs have numerous mild-to-severe side effects. Thus, there is a need for safe, effective, affordable, and alternative anti-inﬂammatory drugs. This study aimed to evaluate the anti-inﬂammatory effect of sclareol (SCL), a diterpene alcohol that is the principal ingredient in the reﬁned oil of Salvia sclarea (L.), through in vivo and in silico studies. First, we examined the individual and combined effects of SCL (5, 10, and 20 mg/kg) and standard drugs celecoxib (CXB) or ketoprofen (KPN) at 42 mg/kg (p.o.) on the formalin-induced inﬂammatory Swiss mice. Additionally, an in silico analysis was conducted to evaluate the potential antiinﬂammatory mechanism of this study. For this, we examined the potentiality of SCL and standards to interact with cyclooxygenase (COX)-1 and COX-2 receptors. Our ﬁndings suggest that SCL exhibits a dose-dependent antiinﬂammatory effect in mice. SCL-20 mg/kg signiﬁcantly reduced the number of paw licks and paw edema diameters. Moreover, SCL-20 combined with CXB-42 and KPN-42 demonstrated better anti-inﬂammatory effects. In comparison to the standards, SCL revealed a comparable binding interaction with COX-1 and COX-2 receptors in the molecular docking study. Furthermore, SCL displayed remarkable pharmacokinetic characteristics. In conclusion, SCL signiﬁcantly and dose-dependently reduced the number of paw licks and edema diameters in animals. Thus, SCL may be responsible for producing an anti-inﬂammatory effect by interacting with COX-1 and COX-2 receptors.

collagen, leukocytes, or lymphocytes may result from inﬂammation if speciﬁc target removal and supporting tissue repair are not well scheduled [3]. It is often believed that microbial invasion is the primary source of the traditional causes of inﬂammation [4]. According to how long an illness has been prevalent, inﬂammation can be divided into two types: acute inﬂammation and chronic inﬂammation. Acute inﬂammation lasts for a few days or weeks and is the body's initial reaction to damaging Abbreviations: sclareol, SCL; celecoxib, CXB; ketoprofen, KPN; cyclooxygenase, COX; tumor necrosis factor, TNF; interleukin, IL; central nervous system, CNS; prostaglandins, PGs; arachidonic acid, AA; thromboxane, TX; Non-steroidal anti-inﬂammatory drugs, NSAIDs; nuclear factor kappa-light chain-enhancer of activated B cells, NF-κB; mitogen-activated protein kinase, MAPK; activating heme oxygenase-1, HO-1; COVID-19 main protease, MPro; Calcium channel, voltage-dependent, Ltype, alpha 1D subunit, Cav1.3.

* Corresponding author. ** Corresponding author.

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pscia.2023.100029

Received in revised form

Accepted

2773-2169/© 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Shandong University. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http:// creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

Page 2

ances 2 (2024) 100029 stimuli [5]. In contrast, chronic inﬂammation is deﬁned by the simultaneous occurrence of tissue apoptosis and regeneration [6]. Numerous chronic disorders such as asthma, atherosclerosis, diabetes, cancer, myocardial infarction, chronic heart failure, and more have been directly or indirectly associated with chronic inﬂammation [7]. Several mediators that constitute complex regulatory systems work together to coordinate the inﬂammatory response. Signals that initiate the inﬂammatory response are produced by inducers [8]. The mediators modify the functional states of the tissues and organs in a way that enables them to adjust to the circumstances caused by speciﬁc inﬂammation-inducing agent [9]. As a result, a generic inﬂammatory "pathway" is made up of inducers, sensors, mediators, and effectors, each of which controls the nature of the inﬂammatory reaction [10]. Inﬂammatory mediators such as tumor necrosis factor (TNF), interleukin (IL)-1, IL-6, and chemokines are triggered by the activation of pathogen-speciﬁc receptors [11]. In this regard, increased production of inﬂammatory cytokines controls the liver cells’secretion of C-reactive protein and coagulation factors [12]. These proteins stimulate the development of the brain endothelium and aid in the creation of prostaglandins (PGs), which act on the central nervous system (CNS) to start sensations like pain and fever [13]. An important role of PGs in the production of inﬂammatory response is to arise the typical symptoms of acute inﬂammation and dramatically increase their production in inﬂamed tissue [14]. In this context, arachidonic acid (AA), produced from the plasma membrane through phospholipases and processed by the sequential acts of PGG/H synthase or by cyclooxygenase (COX) and their associated synthases, is converted into PGs and thromboxane A2 (TXA2), which are together referred to as prostanoids [15]. The main bioactive PGs produced in vivo include prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), prostacyclin (PGI2), prostaglandin D2 (PGD2), and prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α) [16]. The quantity and degree of PG formation ﬂuctuate signiﬁcantly when an inﬂammatory reaction occurs because, in healthy tissues, PG synthesis is typically quite modest, but it rises quickly during acute inﬂammation [17]. Non-steroidal anti-inﬂammatory drugs (NSAIDs) can inhibit COX-1 and, to a lesser extent, COX-2, which reduce inﬂammation, pain, and heat [18]. However, long-term use of NSAIDs may harm kidneys and stomach mucosa, possibly as a result of diminished cytoprotection by PGE2 [19]. Furthermore, inﬂammation has been linked to several neurodegenerative conditions, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, and many more [20]. Many studies have shown that inﬂammation has positive short-term effects and negative long-term effects in these conditions [21]. For instance, inhibiting inﬂammation with antagonists or speciﬁc gene inhibitors can make a person more vulnerable to infection [22].

Plants are the major source of phytochemicals used in the development of new medications. International businesses are more motivated than ever to look for compounds derived from plant sources as leads for new drugs [23]. The enormous therapeutic potential for treating disorders that impact living things is represented by the structural diversity of plant metabolites [24]. Moreover, there have been complaints that expensive medications with unfavorable side effects are used far too frequently in modern pharmaceutical practice [25]. Thus, natural compounds appear to solve several contemporary health issues with fewer adverse effects [26]. In this context, several phytochemicals found in plants are beneﬁcial in decreasing inﬂammation and have fewer side effects [27]. In addition, as NSAID usage is linked to several adverse effects on kidney and gastrointestinal systems, medicinal herbs are utilized as a substitute for these pharmaceuticals [28]. The major drawback of newly developed synthetic medications is their toxicity and the recurrence of symptoms after withdrawal [29]. As a result, it is essential to investigate and discover medications that have anti-inﬂammatory action, and attempts are being made to discover anti-inﬂammatory compounds from medicinal herbs [30].

Sclareol (SCL, labd-14-ene-8, 13-diol) is a biologically active labdanetype diterpene that is derived from the fresh leaves and ﬂowers of clary sage (Salvia sclarea L.), a plant that is extensively grown for its essential oil, which is a common ingredient in food, cosmetics, and conventional medications [31]. SCL can alter cytokine patterns, which in turn modiﬁes immunological responses [32]. SCL exhibits several important biological activities, where it acts against diabetes and high blood pressure, and exerts effects against tumors, inﬂammation, and harmful microbes [33]. According to early literature, SCL was frequently used in empirical medicine to treat a variety of illnesses, including dysmenorrhea, mouth irritation, arthritis, and ailments of the digestive system [34]. Sclareol has recently been discovered to reduce pulmonary inﬂammation brought on by lipopolysaccharide by inhibiting nuclear factor kappa-light chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB) and mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and activating heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) signaling pathways [35].

Previous research ﬁndings showed that SCL inhibits the growth of a variety of cancer cells, including breast, colon, and lung cancer [36]. In addition, SCL nearly exhibited the same anti-microbial impact as ﬂuconazole against several microorganisms [37]. Moreover, the discovery of SCL as a new Calcium channel, voltage-dependent, L-type, and alpha 1D subunit (Cav1.3) antagonist against Parkinson's disease seems encouraging [38]. SCL has also been regarded as a potential treatment or preventative measure for SARS-CoV-2 by speciﬁcally targeting COVID-19 main protease (MPro) [39]. Additionally, as an F1FO-ATP synthase inhibitor, SCL reduces the number of free radicals produced in retinal rod, suggesting that it may one day be used as a treatment for retinal diseases [40]. This study intends to assess the anti-inﬂammatory effects of SCL in Swiss albino mice as a formalin-induced paw edema model, relying on previous research and SCL's broad range of biological activity. To further comprehend the potential mechanism(s) of action for this antiinﬂammatory activity, in silico investigations were carried out. Even yet, more research is much desired to enable the use of SCL as an antiinﬂammatory drug in clinical trials.

2. Materials and methods 2.1. In vivo study 2.1.1. Chemicals and reagents We bought SCL 98.0% [Cas No. 515-03-7] from Sigma-Aldrich (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany). The standard medications celecoxib (CXB) [Cas No. 169590-42-5] and ketoprofen (KPN) [Cas No. 22071-15-4] were purchased from East West Pharma (India) Private Limited and Eskayef Pharmaceuticals Ltd. (Bangladesh), respectively. Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, Maharashtra, (India) supplied the emulsiﬁer tween-80 [Cas No. 9005-65-6] and the inﬂammatory agent formalin [Cas No. 50-00-0]. The chemical structures of SCL and standards are shown in

Fig. 1.

2.2. Selection and preparation of test/control groups 2.2.1. Experimental animals Forty healthy adult male Swiss albino mice of either sex (24–28 g, 6–7 weeks of age) purchased from the animal house of Jahangirnagar University (Savar, Bangladesh) were used throughout this investigation. These animals were housed in cages at the Pharmacology Lab of Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman Science and Technology University (BSMRSTU), Gopalganj. They were given free access to a standard diet (food and water) ad libitum and were kept under controlled lighting (12 h dark/light cycle), 60–70% moisture, and 26  2C until the test commenced. Animals were randomly divided into eight groups of ﬁve animals each. Drug was administered orally with the aid of a stainlesssteel needle attached to a plastic syringe. The present experiment was conducted from 08:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m., and the animals were monitored for 17 h to check their possible mortality after the study. Experimental design and protocols were approved by the Department of Pharmacy and Animal and Human Ethical Committee of BSMRSTU (#bsmrstu16-11/

Page 3

ances 2 (2024) 100029

Fig. 1. Chemical structure of sclareol and

reference drugs.

22). Details of the groups, treatment, doses, and target receptors are listed in Table 1.

2.2.2. Dose selection for sclareol According to previous research, oral dosages of 5–150 mg/kg of SCL may have a considerable anti-inﬂammatory effect in experimental animals [41]. SCL may be an innovative approach to managing inﬂammatory reactions, as evidenced by another study that shows it to have strong dose-dependent anti-inﬂammatory effects in mice at doses of 2.5, 5, and 10 mg/kg [42]. Additionally, mice given SCL (5 and 10 mg/kg) showed a decrease in skin dermatitis lesions [43]. In this study, we determined the maximum oral dose of SCL to be 5, 10, and 20 mg/kg based on the results of the literature review (safety, toxicity, dosage response, and clinical relevance).

2.2.3. Study design (formalin-induced paw edema in mice) For this study, we used 12-h fasted animals after 7-day acclimation in the laboratory environment. Brieﬂy, a total of forty mice (24–30 g, b.w.) of both sexes were randomly divided into eight groups of ﬁve animals each (Table 1). All the treatments were given via oral gavage (p.o.) 30 min before injection of formalin (0.5% formaldehyde) solution in the sub-plantar area of the right hind paw of the animals. For this, 50 μL of formalin solution prepared in normal saline was injected into each animal. Then the licking behavior was counted for the ﬁrst 1–10 min (early phase) and 21–30 min (late phase), while the paw edema diameter was measured by using a slide caliper at 60, 90, and 120 min after formalin injection. Paw edema was determined compared to the baseline (normal) paw diameter of each animal in millimeters. Then the following parameters were determined.

PED (mm) ¼ Paw diameter in observed time–Baseline paw diameter %RPE ¼ ðPDOTVehicle–PDOTTestÞ ðPDOTVehicleÞ  100 100 Table 1 Name of treatments, their dose, and the target receptor.

Treatment groups Composition Dose Target receptor Gr-I Vehicle (0.5% tween-80 dissolved in normal saline) 10 ml/kg–Gr-II Sclareol (SCL) 5 mg/kg Under investigation Gr-III 10 mg/kg Gr-IV 20 mg/kg Gr-V Celecoxib (CXB) 42 mg/kg COX-2 Gr-VI Ketoprofen (KPN) 42 mg/kg COX-1 Gr-VII SCL-10 þ CXB-42 10 mg/kg þ 42 mg/kg Under investigation Gr-VIII SCL-10 þ KPN-42 10 mg/kg þ 42 mg/kg All treatments are given at 10 ml/kg via oral gavage (p.o.); SCL: Sclareol; CXB: Celecoxib; KPN: Ketoprofen; COX: Cyclooxygenase.

%TRE ¼ (Number of animals with normal paw  Total animals in a group)  100 Where, PED, PDOT, RPE, and TRE mean paw edema diameter, paw diameter in observed time, reduction of paw edema, and total reduction of edema at ﬁnal observation time.

2.2.4. Statistical analysis Results of the anti-inﬂammatory activity are provided as the mean  standard error of the mean (SEM) or percentage values. Statistical analysis was performed with the aid of GraphPad Prism (version 9.5) [44]. Data were subjected to one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), and then the post hoc test of t-Student-Newman-Keuls was used to compare the experimental groups with the vehicle (control) group; differences were considered signiﬁcant at p < 0.05 at the 95% conﬁdence intervals.

2.3. In silico studies 2.3.1. Homology model of Cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) receptors 2.3.1.1. Retrieval of sequences. The protein sequences of COX-1 and COX-2 were retrieved from the UniProt protein repository and then saved in Notepad in the FASTA ﬁle format with the accession ID. The UniProt protein database, which is accessible to the public, contains the amino acid sequences of proteins [45].

2.3.1.2. Model building and evaluation. We conducted homology modeling of the COX-1 and COX-2 receptors using the excellent online program "SWISS-MODEL" [46]. The NCBI BLAST program [47] was used to perform a BLAST analysis to identify the most suitable templates. The stereochemical properties of homology models were assessed using the Φ-Ψ Ramachandran plot [48]. Moreover, the PROCHECK approach with the PDBsum server was used to verify the accuracy and validity of the models [49].

2.3.2. Protein preparation In protein preparation, macromolecules were reﬁned by removing extraneous amino acid residues and water molecules with PyMOL version 1.7.4.5 Edu [50]. Thereafter, the energy consumption of the protein structure was reduced using the Swiss-PDB Viewer software package (version 4.1.0) with the GROMOS 96 43B1 parameters set [51].

2.3.3. Ligand preparation The 3D chemical structures of standard drugs CXB and KPN (Pub-Chem ID: 3825 and 2662, respectively) and SCL (PubChem ID: 163263) were obtained from the PubChem database in the 'sdf' format [52]. We minimized the ligands using Chem3D Pro 20.1.1 software applications providing Allinger's force ﬁeld (MM2) method [53].

2.3.4. Docking protocol and non-bond interactions Molecular docking simulation is a computerized method for drug

Page 4

ances 2 (2024) 100029 design utilized in pharmaceutical research. This approach is used to assess the pharmacodynamic qualities of active compounds by evaluating and aligning molecules to speciﬁc binding sites using PyRx v0.8 virtual screening software [54]. The docking output establishes the ligand binding intensity at the speciﬁc protein's active site. In this technique, kcal/mol is normally used as a unit when the ligand's binding afﬁnity for a negative score is calculated [55]. On the other hand, BIOVIA Discovery Studio v21.1.0 is used to investigate the active binding areas of the proteins for analyzing non-bond interactions between ligand-protein complexes [56].

2.3.5. Pharmacokinetics and drug-likeness properties Statistical analysis of pharmacokinetics examines how well the body reacts to medications given over time, referred to as ADMET properties. In this regard, the in silico technique is essential because it helps a chemical become a viable medicine by studying its preliminary pharmacokinetic properties. The qualitative parameter known as "drug-likeness" is employed in study and development to determine how precisely a chemical resembles a pharmaceutical in diverse situations. To test the ADMET functions and drug-likeness characteristics of SCL and the standards, the SwissADME database [57], the pkCSM web program [58] and the publicly accessible ProTox-II servers [59] were used.

3. Results 3.1. In vivo study 3.1.1. Formalin-induced paw edema in mice A typical experimental model in pharmacology research is Formalininduced paw edema in Swiss mice. This model is intended for use in the research of inﬂammation, particularly acute inﬂammatory reactions [60]. Formalin is used to cause inﬂammation in the hind paws of mice so that researchers may study several aspects of inﬂammation and evaluate if certain test substances or medications have any anti-inﬂammatory properties [61]. The results of our investigation on how SCL affects mice's paw licking are displayed in Table 2. It showed that the standard drugs CXB (42 mg/kg) and KPN (42 mg/kg) as well as SCL dose-dependently and signiﬁcantly (p < 0.05) decreased the number of paw licks in both phases compared to the control (vehicle) group. SCL reduced the licking parameter more effectively when combined with CXB or KPN (Gr-VII and Gr-VIII) in all phases. Moreover, SCL at 20 mg/kg or its combination with KPN signiﬁcantly (p < 0.05) reduced paw licking more than CXB alone (Gr-V) or co-treated (Gr-VII) with it (Table 2). Results also indicated that the maximal dose of the SCL-20 group exhibits better activity (0.40  0.22 mm) as compared to the groups given vehicle, CXB, and KPN, which exhibited PED values of (2.00  0.00, 0.70  0.34, and 0.40  0.22 mm, respectively) for 120 min.

Table 2 The number of paw licking observed in test and/or control groups.

Treatment groups Number of licking Early phase (1–10 min) Late phase (21–30 min) Gr-I 42.20  2.10 22.40  2.17 Gr-II 15.60  1.99*b 5.40  1.93*b Gr-III 13.80  2.16*b 3.60  1.58*b Gr-IV 10.80  0.96*ab 1.60  1.27*ab Gr-V 16.00  2.50* 6.20  2.13* Gr-VI 8.60  2.86*ab 1.40  1.03*ab Gr-VII 10.40  0.84*ab 1.60  1.01*ab Gr-VIII 7.40  0.84*ab 1.10  0.93*ab Values are the mean  standard error of the mean (SEM) (n ¼ 5); One-way ANOVA followed by t-Student–Newman–Keuls's as post hoc test at 95% conﬁdence intervals; *: P < 0.05 compared to the Vehicle group; a: p < 0.05 compared to the Gr-III; b: p < 0.05 compared to Gr-V; Gr-I: Vehicle; Gr-II: Sclareol (SCL) 5 mg/kg; Gr-III: SCL 10 mg/kg; Gr-IV: SCL 20 mg/kg; Gr-V: Celecoxib (CXB) 42 mg/kg; Gr-VI: Ketoprofen (KPN) 42 mg/kg; Gr-VII: SCL-10 þ CXB-42; Gr-VIII: SCL-10 þ KPN-42.

Furthermore, data in Table 3 indicate that the combined groups have smaller paw edema diameters than the NC group along with the standards, and the (SCL þ KPN) group has the smallest PED (0.10  0.11 mm), which was the lowest observed. Similarly, an elevated reduction in paw edema (RPE) (80%) for 120 min compared to NC was observed for the highest dose of SCL-20. Additionally, the (SCL þ KPN) group had the highest RPE value (95%) among the combined groups. However, this group also showed the maximum TRE value (80%) for 120 min. Moreover, results in Table 4 indicate that the SCL-10 and SCL-20 exhibit TRE values of 40 and 60%, respectively, which are similar to the standards CXB and KPN.

According to our in vivo ﬁndings, SCL signiﬁcantly and dosedependently decreased the number of licking in each group when compared to the vehicle group and improved the paw edema diameter over time when compared to the control groups.

3.2. In silico studies 3.2.1. Homology model of Cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) receptors Homology modeling is one of the major computerized structural prediction methods used in developing 3D models of protein sequences employing experimentally veriﬁed structures of relevant family members as templates [62]. It is regarded as one of the most precise techniques and is crucial in improving, simplifying, lowering the cost, and enhancing the utility of the drug discovery process [63]. Within this context, the inﬂammatory receptors COX-1 and COX-2 have amino acid sequences available in the UniProt database (UniProt accession IDs: P00395 and P00403, respectively). The NCBI Blast Algorithm was subsequently utilized to select the closest homologous templates of COX-1 and COX-2, which were generated by the SWISS-MODEL web server.

Fig. 2 depicts

the three-dimensional homology model of the inﬂammatory receptors.

The Ramachandran plot

(Fig. 3), obtained by the PROCHECK program

[64], was applied to verify the homology models. Thereafter, the Swiss-PDB Viewer application (v4.1.0) [65] was employed to optimize the inﬂammatory models before molecular docking simulation. On the other hand, the Ramachandran plot is important in determining how stereochemically accurate the protein model is. The conformation angles (Φ-Ψ), also referred to as the Ramachandran angles, are responsible for determining the framework of the polypeptide chain in a protein molecule. The Ramachandran plot, which compares these angles against one another to identify the range of permissible and undesirable torsion angle values, is used to evaluate the quality of threedimensional protein structures. As glycine residues are not restricted to the plot regions speciﬁed for the other side chain groups, triangles are used to represent them. The intensity and color of the map correspond to Table 3 Paw edema diameter observed in test and/or control groups.

Treatment groups Edema diameter (mm) 60 min 90 min 120 min Gr-I 2.20  0.22 1.80  0.22 2.00  0.00 Gr-II 1.70  0.22* 1.00  0.25*b 0.50  0.18*b Gr-III 1.50  0.25*b 0.90  0.27*b 0.50  0.25*b Gr-IV 1.30  0.34*abc 0.85  0.43*ab 0.40  0.27*ab Gr-V 1.60  0.27* 1.40  0.27* 0.70  0.34* Gr-VI 1.40  0.27*ab 0.80  0.22*ab 0.40  0.22*ab Gr-VII 1.70  0.29* 0.90  0.21*b 0.40  0.21*ab Gr-VIII 1.50  0.18*b 0.80  0.22*ab 0.10  0.11*abc Values are the mean  standard error of the mean (SEM) (n ¼ 5); One-way ANOVA followed by t-Student–Newman–Keuls's as post hoc test at 95% conﬁdence intervals; *: p < 0.05 compared to the Vehicle group; a: p < 0.05 compared to the Gr-III; b: p < 0.05 compared to Gr-V; c: p < 0.05 compared to Gr-VI; Gr-I: Vehicle; Gr-II: Sclareol (SCL) 5 mg/kg; Gr-III: SCL 10 mg/kg; Gr-IV: SCL 20 mg/ kg; Gr-V: Celecoxib (CXB) 42 mg/kg; Gr-VI: Ketoprofen (KPN) 42 mg/kg; Gr-VII: SCL-10 þ CXB-42; Gr-VIII: SCL-10 þ KPN-42.

Page 5

ances 2 (2024) 100029 Table 4 Paw edema reduction proﬁles in test and/or control groups.

3.2.2. Interaction of sclareol (SCL) and ketoprofen (KPN) with COX-1 receptor The degree of interaction that exists between a drug candidate and its target (such as a protein, enzyme, or receptor) is referred to as binding afﬁnity in pharmaceutical research [66]. The measurement of binding afﬁnity aids in the identiﬁcation of possible medications with high efﬁcacy and speciﬁcity [67]. Furthermore, non-bond interactions such as hydrogen bonds or others, are essential for maintaining the stability of drug-receptor complex structures. These interactions maintain the integrity of biological systems [68]. Results from this study revealed that SCL demonstrates a high binding afﬁnity for the inﬂammatory COX-1 receptor. The docking score of SCL with COX-1 was–7.5 kcal/mol, which is impressive when compared to the docking value of–8.4 kcal/mol for the conventional KPN (Table 5). SCL interacts with COX-1 through six alkyl bonds and one pi-alkyl bond with Tyr136. On the other hand, KPN exhibited binding interactions with COX-1 through two H-bonds (conventional and carbon), two pi-pi stacked bonds, one alkyl bond with Leu20, four pi-alkyl bonds, and one pi-sulfur bond with Met468. The non-bond interactions between SCL and KPN with the COX-1 receptor are illustrated in

Fig. 4 in both 2D and 3D conﬁgurations.

Treatment groups Edema diameter (%) TRE (%) 60 min 90 min 120 min 120 min Gr-I 0.00 0.00 0.00 0.00 Gr-II 22.72 44.44 75.00 20.00 Gr-III 31.82 50.00 75.00 40.00 Gr-IV 40.91 52.78 80.00 60.00 Gr-V 27.27 22.22 65.00 40.00 Gr-VI 36.36 55.56 80.00 60.00 Gr-VII 22.72 50.00 80.00 40.00 Gr-VIII 31.82 55.56 95.00 80.00 Values are expressed as percentages compared to the vehicle group; TRE: Total edema reduction at ﬁnal observation time. Gr-I: Vehicle; Gr-II: Sclareol (SCL) 5 mg/kg; Gr-III: SCL 10 mg/kg; Gr-IV: SCL 20 mg/kg; Gr-V: Celecoxib (CXB) 42 mg/kg; Gr-VI: Ketoprofen (KPN) 42 mg/kg; Gr-VII: SCL-10 þ CXB-42; Gr-VIII: SCL-10 þ KPN-42.

the various regions that have been explored; the "core" regions, or darkest regions, are displayed in red and correspond to the most beneﬁcial Φ-Ψ value combinations. Idealistically, more than 90% of the leftovers would be located in these "core" regions. The most dependable indicators of stereochemical integrity are the proportion of residues that are concentrated in the "core" regions. According to Ramachandran plot statistics, the residues in the most ideal regions for COX-1 and COX-2 are approximately 96.65% and 96.00%, respectively.

3.2.3. Interaction of sclareol (SCL) and celecoxib (CXB) with COX-2 receptor For the inﬂammatory COX-2 receptor, SCL exhibited exceptional binding afﬁnity. SCL has a remarkable docking result with COX-2 of–7.2 kcal/mol, which is comparable to the standard CXB's docking value

Fig. 2. Homology model of inﬂammatory receptors using the SWISS-MODEL (A) COX-1 and (B) COX-2.

Fig. 3. The optimized models of inﬂammatory receptors (A) COX-1 and (B) COX-2 via SWISS-MODEL.

Page 6

ances 2 (2024) 100029 Table 5 Best binding afﬁnity results and non-bond interactions of SCL and KPN with inﬂammatory receptor COX-1.

Protein (Receptor) Binding afﬁnity (kcal/mol) Number of H-Bond H-bond residues Bond length (Å) Other bond residues SCL and COX-1 7.5 0––Pro131 Pro131 Pro222 Ala223 Pro222 Pro131 Tyr136 KPN and COX-1 8.4 2 Phe397 Leu21 2.60 2.59 Phe393 Phe393 Leu20 Leu20 Ala24 Ile73 Ile472 of–7.6 kcal/mol (Table 6). Moreover, SCL interacts with COX-2 via one conventional H-bond with Ser94, three pi-alkyl bonds, and eight alkyl bonds. In contrast, CXB revealed binding interactions with COX-2 through three H-bonds (two conventional and one carbon), one pi-pi Tshaped bond with Phe219, two alkyl bonds, and three pi-alkyl bonds. The non-bond interactions between SCL and CXB with the COX-2 receptor are illustrated in

Fig. 5 in 2D and 3D conﬁgurations.

In this investigation, SCL displayed non-bonding residues similar to those of CXB with COX-2. However, no comparable residues were found in the interaction between SCL and COX-1.

3.2.4. Drug-likeness properties and pharmacokinetics "Drug-likeness" is employed to describe the harmony between structural features and other molecular properties that regulate the formation

Fig. 4. The 2D and 3D structure of molecular docking interactions of (A) SCL and (B) KPN with COX-1 receptor.

of novel medications. The ﬁve Lipinski rules are the primary determinants in the creation of new medicines. This involves the oral bioavailability of a substance and assists in determining the degree of absorption or permeability of lipid bilayers that exist within the human body. In our research, we employed SwissADME to predict the Lipinski criteria of the compounds. The following criteria must be fulﬁlled by drugs: molecular weight 500 Da, hydrogen bond donor groups 5, hydrogen bond acceptor groups 10, high lipophilicity value (log  P5), and a molar refractivity of 40–130 [69].

Based on these conditions, SCL may be classiﬁed as a drug-like substance (Table 7). Since it plays a signiﬁcant role in drug discovery and development, a thorough examination of pharmacokinetic data is essential for the therapeutic use of drugs. Moreover, a compound must meet certain criteria to be designated as a medicine. In this study, we employed pkCSM and ProTox-II online tools to predict the pharmacokinetic parameters or ADMET properties of SCL compared with the standards. Listed in Table 8 are the pharmacokinetic properties (ADMET) of SCL and standards.

4. Discussion Inﬂammation is the immunological reaction to infections or damage and has been associated with the pathogenesis and progression of rheumatoid arthritis, cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and neurological diseases and disorders [70]. An essentially favorable process, inﬂammation eliminates irritating elements and repairs damaged tissues and physiological function [71]. The immediate inﬂow of granulocytes, usually neutrophils, quickly followed by monocytes, which develop into inﬂammatory macrophages and then multiply and alter the activities of local tissue macrophages, are the hallmarks of the acute stage of inﬂammation [72]. The four essential symptoms of acute inﬂammation are rubor (redness), calor (heat), tumor (swelling), and dolor (pain) [73]. The inﬂammatory response caused by harmful stimuli can be reduced

Page 7

ances 2 (2024) 100029 Table 6 Best binding afﬁnity results and non-bond interactions of SCL and CXB with inﬂammatory receptor COX-2.

number of macrophages and lymphocytes reverts to normal levels [75]. Instead of the inﬂammation persisting and becoming dysfunctional, which can result in scars and organ dysfunction, the acute inﬂammatory reaction often successfully resolves and recovers tissue damage [76]. Hence, it may be expected that chronic dysplastic inﬂammation, severe tissue injury, and autoimmunity would be more likely to develop if acute inﬂammation persists [15].

PGG/H synthases sometimes referred to as COXs, are bifunctional enzymes that possess both COX and peroxidase action [77]. They come in two different isoforms, COX-1 and COX-2, and their activity is essential for the formation of PGs [78]. Most cells produce COX-1, and it is the major generator of prostanoids that support administrative activities including gastric epithelial cytoprotection and homeostasis [79]. In inﬂammation in regenerative disorders like cancer, COX-2 is the most signiﬁcant source of prostanoid production because it is triggered by inﬂammatory stimulants, hormones, and growth factors [80]. Nonetheless, both COX-1 and COX-2 can play a role in releasing prostanoids during inﬂammation and help produce autoregulatory and homeostatic prostanoids [81]. However, COX-2 seems to be the main catalyst to produce PGs during inﬂammation, and there is certain evidence that indicates both isoforms may be involved in the acute inﬂammatory response [77]. Furthermore, it is known that COX-1 is induced during the inﬂammatory response caused by LPS and cellular development, and the Protein (Receptor) Binding afﬁnity (kcal/mol) Number of H-Bond H-bond residues Bond length (Å) Other bond residues SCL and COX-2 7.2 1 Ser94 2.50 Ala148 Pro223 Leu95 Leu95 Pro145 Leu116 Pro223 Pro223 Tyr110 Phe219 Phe219 CXB and COX-2 7.6 3 Gly114 Gly114 Ser94 2.11 2.48 2.85 Phe219 Leu95 Pro145 Phe219 Pro223 Leu95 and eventually resolved through a process called phagocytosis [74]. Granulocytes are removed when the inﬂammation diminishes, and the

Fig. 5. The 2D and 3D structure of molecular docking interactions of (A) SCL and (B) CXB with COX-1 receptor.

Table 7 Lipinski's rule of ﬁve analyses of SCL and standards.

Ligands Formula Molecular Weight (g/ mol) H-Bond donors (HBDs) H-Bond acceptors (HBAs) log Po/w (XLOGP3) Molar refractivity Sclareol C20H36O2 308.50 2 2 4.93 95.43 Celecoxib C17H14F3N3O2S 381.37 1 7 3.40 89.96 Ketoprofen C16H14O3 254.28 1 3 3.12 72.67

Page 8

ances 2 (2024) 100029 Table 8 Pharmacokinetic properties (ADMET) of SCL and standards.

encompasses the inﬂammatory responses facilitated by several substances, including PGs, bradykinin, serotonin (5HT), histamine, and cytokines such as IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, eicosanoids, and NO [88,89]. Formalin causes discomfort; however, this irritation also causes a localized inﬂammatory response, including pain. Additionally, the injection of formalin causes edema and increases vascular permeability [90]. The edema and inﬂammatory responses in the formalin test are related to different chemicals and enzymes. The initial investigations employing the formalin test in rats without any pathological conditions revealed an elevation in the release of NO and PGs within the spinal cord. These biochemical changes were found to have a role in the development of spinal sensitization, which was facilitated by the activation of glutamatergic NMDA receptors [91–93]. In this study, SCL and the standards CXB and KPN signiﬁcantly reduced both phases of licking in animals. However, SCL co-treated with KPN showed better effects than the combined CXB group.

Recent research examined the anti-inﬂammatory properties of labdane-type diterpenes in paw edema models [94]. In this regard, researchers used carrageenan and formalin-induced rat paw edema models to assess the anti-inﬂammatory properties of polyalthic acid, a labdane-type diterpenoid. These people found that polyalthic acid substantially and dose-dependently decreases edema at all dosages used (10–178 mg/kg). The lowest dosage (10 mg/kg) displayed the smallest anti-inﬂammatory activity (29.86%). The effects of the 100 and 178 mg/kg dosages of polyalthic acid exhibited 70.9% and 82.4%, respectively, which were noticeably larger than the 47.4% result achieved with the 32 mg/kg dose of the well-known drug naproxen [95]. Another study used the paw model of λ-carrageenan (Carr)-induced edema to investigate the anti-inﬂammatory effects of SCL. Results revealed that 10 mg/kg of indomethacin (Indo) and 10 mg/kg of SCL effectively prevented the development of edema 3 h after treatment (p < 0.001 or < 0.01). The fact that SCL (10 mg/kg) exerted comparable effects to Indo (10 mg/kg) in terms of its ability to reduce the production of the iNOS and COX-2 proteins showed that SCL may have an anti-inﬂammatory mechanism involving the inhibition of these proteins [96]. Our in vivo investigation found that SCL at the highest dose (20 mg/kg, p.o.) caused the lowest number of paw licks and improved PED and RPE values in formalin-induced inﬂammatory mice, which is consistent with the anti-inﬂammatory effects shown in previous studies. A synergistic effect occurs when two or more processes interact, resulting in an effect that surpasses the total effect of those processes when employed individually [97]. In our in vivo study, the test compound SCL exhibited signiﬁcant synergistic effects with the standard drugs, as the number of licks and paw edema diameter of the animals in combined groups reduced with increasing time compared to the animals in standard groups. Taken together, SCL remarkably dose-dependently reduced the number of licking in the different groups of SCL-treated animals compared to the vehicle group. Furthermore, SCL also improved the paw edema diameter time-dependently compared to the control groups, indicating its swelling reduction capacity.

The search for innovative medicines and their manufacture takes much time and effort. As a component of computer-aided drug design (CADD), in silico molecular docking has grown in prominence recently [98]. The primary beneﬁt of in silico drug design is that it substantially lowers the expenses of drug research and development. This strategy can relate signiﬁcantly to each stage of drug production, from idea to conclusion [99]. A wide range of in silico approaches combine and draw inspiration from various aspects of foundational research and application [100]. Results from our in silico investigation indicate that SCL has a strong docking potential for interaction with COX-1 (–7.5 kcal/mol) and COX-2 (–7.2 kcal/mol) receptors. While some earlier studies suggest that SCL interacts with a variety of targets to inhibit inﬂammatory processes through COX pathways, they did not provide any reliable evidence to support their selective action [101,102]. In this study, SCL exhibited similar non-bonding residues like CXB with COX-2 through H-bond (Ser94), Pi-Alkyl (Phe219) bond, and alkyl (Leu95 and Pro145) bonds Properties Factors SCL CXB KFN Absorption (A) Aqueous solubility (log mol/ L) 4.988 4.45 2.96 Caco-2 permeability (log Papp in 10-6 cm/s) 1.60 0.83 1.27 Intestinal absorption (% Absorbed) 90.824 92.995 100 Distribution (D) VDss (human) (log L/kg) 0.382 0.273 1.944 Blood-brain barrier (log BB) 0.073 0.931 0.162 CNS Permeability (log PS) 2.517 2.052 2.156 Metabolism (M) CYP2D6 inhibitor No No No CYP3A4 inhibitor No Yes No Excretion (E) Total clearance (log ml/min/ kg) 0.914 0.435 0.286 Toxicity (T) Hepatotoxicity No Yes No AMES toxicity No No No Skin sensitization No No No Max. tolerated dose (human) (log mg/kg/day) 0.489 0.178 0.71 hERG I inhibitor No No No hERG II inhibitor No No No Oral rat acute toxicity (LD50) (mol/kg) 1.848 1.975 2.174 Carcinogenicity Inactive Active Inactive Immunotoxicity Inactive Inactive Inactive Mutagenicity Inactive Inactive Inactive Cytotoxicity Inactive Inactive Inactive Data in Table 8 reveal that all calculated factors related to SCL, including aqueous solubility, Caco-2 permeability, intestinal absorption, VDss (human), blood-brain barrier, CNS permeability, CYP family metabolism, total clearance, hepatotoxicity, AMES toxicity test, skin sensitization, maximum tolerated dose (MTD), human ether-a-go-go-related gene (hERG) I and II inhibitors, and oral rat acute toxicity (LD50), are within the allowable limits. Additional toxicity proﬁles, such as carcinogenicity, immunotoxicity, mutagenicity, and cytotoxicity, have also been considered remarkable.

enzyme is mainly expressed in local inﬂammatory cells [82]. According to human research, the ﬁrst stage of acute inﬂammation is driven by COX-1-derived mediators, whereas COX-2 overexpression occurs a few hours later [83]. Unfortunately, large-scale controlled clinical studies were never carried out to compare the effectiveness of NSAIDs that suppress both COXs (e.g., COX-1 and COX-2) and COX-2 independently. Instead of comparing clinical effectiveness, these experiments were developed to identify differences in the frequency of GI side effects [15]. Male experimental animals are used in most preclinical in vivo research to prevent hormonal interference with the treatments. However, because of these male biases, different dosages, dose frequencies, pharmacological, and pharmacokinetic characteristics may be used for a whole population. For instance, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) reports that ten prescription medications caused signiﬁcant adverse effects in women between 1997 and 2000. Therefore, it is essential to evaluate a new medication candidate employing both sexed animals in a preclinical inquiry to achieve exact and reproducible biological outcomes applicable to both men and women [84]. For this reason, we utilized Swiss albino mice of both sexes in the current investigation.

The formalin test is a frequently employed model of inﬂammatory and chronic pain [85]. Formalin is an aqueous (water) 40% solution of formaldehyde, a smelly gas that can be used as an antiseptic, disinfectant, and histological tissue ﬁxative [86]. Infusion of formalin into a rodent's paw causes an inﬂammatory response that results in edema of the affected area [87]. In our study. injection of formalin caused a biphasic sensation of pain. The ﬁrst stage (maximum intensity 5 min after the administration of formalin) is believed to be induced by the direct activation of primary afferent sensory neurons and causes neurogenic pain, while the second stage (occurring between 15 and 30 min after formalin injection) has been suggested as the result of the interaction between afferent input and the dorsal horn's central sensitization which

Page 9

ances 2 (2024) 100029

Fig. 6. Possible anti-inﬂammatory mechanism of sclareol. Microsoft PowerPoint (Microsoft Ofﬁce Professional Plus 2019) was employed to create this possible

mechanism including the‘Phospholipid Membrane’and other artworks.

whereas no identical residues were detected in the interaction between SCL and COX-1. As a result, even though further study is required, we might hypothesize that SCL may be a possible selective COX-2 inhibitor. Furthermore, SCL's drug-like features have no violations of the Lipinski criteria identiﬁed by SwissADME, indicating that it has good absorption qualities. As compared to the standards, the ADMET scores of SCL obtained from pkCSM and ProTox II servers are also impressive. Taken together, SCL demonstrated a signiﬁcant anti-inﬂammatory effect in animal models.

Arachidonic acid was initially identiﬁed to be metabolized by the COXs, which produce prostanoids such as TXA2 and PGs. Phospholipases must release the lipid from the plasma membrane for this to happen, and the COX enzymes must then metabolize it into PGG2 and PGH2 [103]. Primarily, prostanoids that support household tasks originate from COX-1 [104]. Conversely, COX-2 is the more signiﬁcant source of prostanoid production in inﬂammation which is triggered by inﬂammatory stimuli, injuries, cell damage, etc. [105]. They might cause cytokine storm syndrome by stimulating monocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells with a high amount of pro-inﬂammatory cytokines (e.g. IL-6, IL-1β, IFN-γ, TNF-α, COX-2, iNOS, NF-κB, and T reg cells) [106]. Certainly, NSAIDs inhibit the COX enzymes that the body needs to produce prostaglandins and as a result, contribute to the reduction of inﬂammation by lowering prostaglandin synthesis [107]. Furthermore, the anti-inﬂammatory properties of SCL have been identiﬁed in earlier research [42,102]. More particular, SCL signiﬁcantly reduced the phosphorylation of the MAPK pathway, which in turn reduced the production of pro-inﬂammatory cytokines like TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-17. Additionally, through the Erk-Jnk, MAPK, iNOS, and/or COX pathways, as well as T cell proliferation and cytokine production, SCL operates on a variety of targets to suppress inﬂammatory activity [108]. Our hypothesis for this mechanism

(Fig. 6) is based on previous analysis and our ﬁndings (both

in vivo and in silico), suggesting that SCL may function similarly to other standard drugs and play an important role in reducing inﬂammation.

5. Conclusions In conclusion, the use of medicinal plants is becoming more common in the treatment of a variety of illnesses, including inﬂammation, due to their low cost and few side effects. Furthermore, several diterpenoids of the labdane class may be selected as lead compounds due to their signiﬁcant anti-inﬂammatory characteristics. Findings from this investigation suggest that SCL reduces inﬂammation in formalin-induced inﬂammatory mice models. Paw-licking and edema diameters were signiﬁcantly decreased (p < 0.05) when SCL-20 was used alone or combined with standards. Also, SCL exhibited better-combined effects with KPN than CXB. Additionally, the in silico study revealed excellent binding afﬁnity with both COX receptors and the pharmacokinetic activity of SCL. We assume that SCL has a dynamic role in antiinﬂammatory activity and may be therapeutically effective. However, more work that could involve human subjects is required to establish the safety and efﬁcacy proﬁle of this compound before it hits the market as a prescribed drug.
